authoritySource: Dr. Ahmed Hesham — OET Speaking Practice Rulebook for Healthcare Professionals (Physiotherapy) v1

rules.title: Open with greeting + identity + role
rules.body: Every OET role-play opens with a greeting, your name, and your role.
exemplarPhrases: Good morning, I'm Dr. Ahmed, one of the doctors on the ward today.

rules.title: Elicit before explaining (ICE)
rules.body: Use ICE (Ideas, Concerns, Expectations) before giving explanation. Ask at least one open question about what the patient already knows or worries about.
exemplarPhrases: What do you understand about the results so far?
What's worrying you most at the moment?

rules.title: Explain in plain English
rules.body: Never use unexplained medical jargon with patients. Define terms immediately when needed.
exemplarPhrases: Hypertension means your blood pressure is higher than we'd like.

rules.title: Check understanding (teach-back)
rules.body: After explaining, invite the patient to repeat in their own words.
exemplarPhrases: Just to check I've explained this well — what will you tell your partner when you get home?

rules.title: Close with plan + safety-net
rules.body: Never close without a clear next step, when to return, and confirming any questions.
exemplarPhrases: Is there anything else you'd like to ask before you go?

rules.title: Acknowledge emotion before information
rules.body: When a patient expresses worry, fear, or sadness, acknowledge it before moving to information.
exemplarPhrases: That sounds really worrying — I can hear how anxious this has made you.

rules.title: Use the patient's name
rules.body: Use the patient's name at least twice in a 5-minute encounter.

rules.title: Formal spoken register
rules.body: Professional but warm. No slang. 'Yeah' acceptable once; 'gonna'/'wanna' are not.

rules.title: Mirror the patient's language
rules.body: If the patient uses a lay term, you may use it too to show listening.

rules.title: Ping-pong turn structure
rules.body: No learner turn should exceed ~120 consecutive words before inviting a response.

rules.title: Invite the patient in
rules.body: Use phrases that hand the turn back to the patient.
exemplarPhrases: Does that make sense so far?

rules.title: No interruption during patient emotion
rules.body: Do not interrupt the patient when they are expressing distress.

rules.title: Start with open questions
rules.body: Begin history-taking with open questions.
exemplarPhrases: Tell me a bit more about how this started.

rules.title: Narrow to closed questions
rules.body: After open questions, narrow with closed questions to confirm specifics.

rules.title: Avoid leading questions
rules.body: Do not lead the patient.

rules.title: One question at a time
rules.body: Avoid stacking multiple questions in one turn.

rules.title: Signpost transitions
rules.body: Signpost when you change topic. Rewarded heavily in OET Appropriateness.
exemplarPhrases: Thank you for that. I'd like to move on to…

rules.title: Chunk information
rules.body: Deliver explanations in chunks of 2–3 sentences.

rules.title: Use analogies
rules.body: Analogies help patients grasp clinical concepts.

rules.title: Natural speech rate 130–160 wpm
rules.body: Target 130–160 words per minute.

rules.title: Link function words
rules.body: Natural connected speech.

rules.title: Sentence stress on key words
rules.body: Stress the key clinical word in each sentence.

rules.title: Contractions OK in speech
rules.body: Contractions are NATURAL in spoken English and EXPECTED in OET speaking. Do NOT carry the written-letter no-contractions rule into speech.

rules.title: Conditionals for uncertainty
rules.body: Use first and second conditionals to express uncertainty.
exemplarPhrases: If your symptoms worsen, please come back.

rules.title: Modal verbs for softening
rules.body: Use modals to soften instructions and options.
exemplarPhrases: You might want to try reducing your salt intake.

rules.title: Active voice for instructions
rules.body: Instructions to the patient should be in the active voice.

rules.title: Stay in role
rules.body: Never break character to discuss the exam or comment meta-linguistically.

rules.title: Respect scenario objectives
rules.body: Every scenario lists objectives the candidate must cover. All must be addressed before closing.

rules.title: No meta comments
rules.body: Do not turn to the examiner to ask 'is that right?'

rules.title: Adapt to patient's emotional state
rules.body: If the patient is anxious, slow down and use more empathy.

rules.title: Red-flag escalation
rules.body: Red-flag symptoms MUST be acknowledged and escalated in-scenario.

rules.title: Safety-net before closing
rules.body: Always state explicit safety-netting before closing.
exemplarPhrases: If the pain returns or you become breathless, please dial 999.

rules.title: ISBAR — Identify
rules.body: Begin handover with Identify: your name, role, the patient's name, and the ward/location.
exemplarPhrases: I'm Dr. Ahmed, the night doctor. I'd like to hand over Mr Patel in Bay 3.

rules.title: ISBAR — Situation
rules.body: State the current situation in one sentence.

rules.title: ISBAR — Background
rules.body: Relevant history: admission reason, comorbidities, key medications, key investigations.

rules.title: ISBAR — Assessment
rules.body: Your clinical assessment.

rules.title: ISBAR — Recommendation
rules.body: Clear recommendation / ask of the receiving clinician.
exemplarPhrases: Could you please review the repeat troponin at 0600?

rules.title: Invite questions at the end
rules.body: Handover closes with: 'Do you have any questions?'

rules.title: Limit filler words
rules.body: Minimise 'um', 'uh', 'like', 'you know'.

rules.title: Prefer pauses over fillers
rules.body: A brief pause is better than 'um'.

rules.title: Summarise the plan before closing
rules.body: Summarise the plan in 2–3 sentences.

rules.title: Offer written information
rules.body: Offer written information where appropriate.
